overall_summary: Chevron, as both a large energy user and a natural gas generation developer, urges FERC to act quickly on large load interconnection reform, endorsing a NOPR that tracks the DOE ANOPR's fourteen principles while allowing regional flexibility and avoiding jurisdictional disputes with states. Chevron specifically supports expedited co-location interconnection processes, interim regional fast-track mechanisms modeled on SPP and MISO ERAS orders, and potential use of FPA section 215 reliability authority to broaden the legal foundation for FERC action.

quotes.text: Chevron encourages the Commission to seize this opportunity to facilitate the expedited interconnection of large loads and generation to support it, including co-located generation.
quotes.concern: Chevron asks FERC to move fast on large load and co-location interconnection

quotes.text: the Commission should take a practical approach that avoids disputes over jurisdictional issues or regional variations and that provides regulatory certainty as soon as practicable to support major near-term generation investment decisions.
quotes.concern: Chevron wants FERC to sidestep jurisdictional fights and give investors certainty quickly

quotes.text: Chevron wants to invest billions of dollars to build the power generation needed to enable America's AI industry. uut the private sector needs consistent policy frameworks to make large-scale investments at the speed required to meet today's power needs and to ensure that the United States remains a technological and energy leader.
quotes.concern: Chevron frames large-scale natural gas investment as contingent on stable FERC policy

quotes.text: The Commission should move forward with a rulemaking that generally accords with, and further develops, the fourteen principles set forth in the DOE ANOPR.
quotes.concern: Chevron supports issuing a NOPR that builds on the DOE ANOPR's fourteen principles

quotes.text: The Commission should set an expedited timetable for NOPR comments and do everything practicable to meet the Secretary's April 30, 2026, deadline for final action.
quotes.concern: Chevron urges FERC to hit the DOE deadline of April 30, 2026

quotes.text: The Commission should ensure that the steps it takes in this proceeding prioritize the development of hybrid arrangements that involve major investments in new generation resources to achieve the maximum electric supply buildout necessary for domestic data centers as soon as practicable.
quotes.concern: Chevron wants co-location hybrid arrangements prioritized to maximize new generation build

quotes.text: New power generation projects for data centers should not wait years in interconnection queues to reach commercial operations.
quotes.concern: Chevron opposes multi-year queue delays for data center generation projects

quotes.text: The Commission should be open to such regional fast-track proposals, which may provide mechanisms to connect large loads and generation before DOE ANOPR standards can be practicably established in all regions.
quotes.concern: Chevron supports interim regional fast-track mechanisms ahead of national standards

quotes.text: The Commission should take a similar approach with respect to large load interconnections.
quotes.concern: Chevron wants FERC to allow independent entity variations for large load rules as it does for generation

quotes.text: Allowing for appropriate flexibility should facilitate the adoption of large load interconnection standards by ensuring that such standards can be efficiently and expediently implemented according to each region's needs.
quotes.concern: Chevron argues regional flexibility speeds adoption of national standards

quotes.text: it would maximize near-term benefits to avoid jurisdictional disputes that would threaten to delay the implementation, and the legal durability, of the Commission's directives in this proceeding.
quotes.concern: Chevron warns that jurisdictional fights risk delaying and undermining FERC's action

quotes.text: the Commission should seek to resolve legitimate state concerns while still taking decisive action within its purview to accelerate large load interconnections.
quotes.concern: Chevron asks FERC to coordinate with states without letting state concerns block action

quotes.text: the Commission should use its authority under that provision to the full extent that it deems necessary to address large load interconnections.
quotes.concern: Chevron recommends FERC invoke FPA section 215 reliability authority for large load rules

quotes.text: The Commission should act quickly, while avoiding distracting jurisdictional disputes with states and accommodating constructive regional proposals.
quotes.concern: Chevron's bottom line: speed plus jurisdictional discipline plus regional accommodation

bins.name: Prioritize co-location hybrid arrangements
bins.description: Chevron, as a natural gas generation developer targeting data center co-location, strongly backs FERC action to expedite hybrid co-location interconnections. It wants the rulemaking to maximize new generation buildout tied to data centers and calls on FERC to create a separate, expedited co-located load and generation interconnection process.

bins.name: Expedited study for large load projects
bins.description: Chevron supports fast-tracking interconnection studies for large loads and their supporting generation, endorsing SPP and MISO ERAS orders as models. It argues that multi-year queue waits are unacceptable and that the NOPR should build on the DOE ANOPR's fourteen principles to accelerate study timelines.

bins.name: Align costs with DOE principles
bins.description: Chevron supports developing a rulemaking that accords with the DOE ANOPR's fourteen principles, which include cost allocation and upgrade cost considerations for large load interconnections.

bins.name: Stay within FERC jurisdictional limits
bins.description: Chevron recommends FERC focus on interconnections to interstate transmission facilities and avoid stretching jurisdictional limits that could invite state challenges or legal reversals. It also proposes invoking FPA section 215 reliability authority and using federal-state coordination mechanisms to broaden the legal foundation while managing state concerns.

bins.name: Firm hybrid co-location rights
bins.description: Chevron wants FERC to prioritize hybrid arrangements that pair large loads with new generation, ensuring the rulemaking creates a clear path for co-located projects to interconnect quickly and with regulatory certainty.

bins.name: Allow regional fast-track mechanisms now
bins.description: Chevron endorses FERC openness to regional fast-track proposals from SPP, MISO, and PJM as a bridge to connect large loads and generation before national standards are finalized in all regions.

bins.name: SPP ERAS as fast-track model
bins.description: Chevron cites the SPP ERAS order and the proposed HILL process as models for expedited large load and generation interconnection, endorsing FERC openness to such regional mechanisms.

bins.name: MISO ERAS as fast-track model
bins.description: Chevron points to the MISO ERAS order as a precedent for accelerated interconnection study processes that can address near-term resource adequacy needs, supporting FERC endorsement of comparable mechanisms.

bins.name: PJM fast-track proposal supported
bins.description: Chevron notes PJM may propose an expedited interconnection process for paired large loads and generation and asks FERC to be open to it as an interim regional mechanism.

bins.name: Regulatory certainty for investment
bins.description: Chevron frames its entire filing around the need for consistent, durable policy frameworks to unlock large-scale private investment in natural gas generation and co-location projects. It argues that jurisdictional uncertainty or prolonged rulemaking delays would undermine investor confidence and slow the infrastructure buildout.

bins.name: Meet April 2026 DOE deadline
bins.description: Chevron urges FERC to set an expedited NOPR comment schedule and do everything practicable to meet the Secretary's April 30, 2026, deadline for final action, treating the timeline as a binding commitment rather than a target.

bins.name: Allow regional independent entity variations
bins.description: Chevron argues FERC should let ISO/RTOs adopt variations on national large load standards suited to regional circumstances, following the same model used in generator interconnection under Orders 2003, 845, and 2023. It contends this accelerates adoption without sacrificing the national baseline.

bins.name: Coordinate with states; do not cede to them
bins.description: Chevron acknowledges state jurisdictional concerns, points to the Tri-State order as evidence FERC already respects retail boundaries, and suggests using the Federal-State Current Issues Collaborative and section 209 joint board mechanisms. However, it insists FERC must still take decisive action within its own purview and not let state objections block progress.

bins.name: Invoke FPA section 215 reliability authority
bins.description: Chevron recommends FERC use its FPA section 215 reliability jurisdiction to reinforce the legal basis for large load interconnection rules, noting that section 215 covers a broader universe of entities including non-jurisdictional entities such as ERCOT participants, federal agencies, and cooperatives.
